Extract Text:
Excerpt from The Musician's Daughter
by Susanne Dunlap
I thought a lot, at the beginning, about my father playing the violin. I still remember the first time I ever saw a concert. I was very small, and we had just come to Esterhaza, a magnificent palace in Sopron, Hungary, when the prince's household moved there from his winter palace, at Eisenstadt, near Vienna. I think it may have been the first concert Papa took part in there. I had a new dress for the occasion, and Mama looked beautiful. I don't think Toby was born yet, or if he was, he was a small baby and stayed in our rooms with a nurse.
The palace at EsterhÃ¡za was so new that it still smelled of raw wood and paint. The rooms were very grand. I had never seen so many candles in one place before. And they were expensive wax candles, not foul-smelling tallow. We stood to the side to watch the fine ladies enter. I thought they looked like dolls; their faces were so perfect. Later my mother told me that they painted them with white lead to make their skin artificially smooth and pale, and then rubbed rouge into their cheeks and lips, and drew lines of charcoal around their eyes so they would appear larger. And the headdresses-in those days the fashion was to have hair that towered up, stuck into place with diamond combs, with ribbons and pearls festooned around. And no matter what color it was naturally, ladies powdered their hair to snowy whiteness, so that the light of the candies was magnified by the glow from the people themselves.
And the men, too, were powdered and patched. Their gold buttons and lace were almost as gaudy as the ladies' finery. But the enormous panniers that held the ladies' skirts out to the side so far that they had to move sideways through doorways fascinated me most of all. I could barely be persuaded to turn my eyes to the orchestra when the concert started.
We were not seated with the guests. In fact, I think we were tucked away in an anteroom. The door was open so we could see through, and everyone pushed me forward to let me watch my father, who looked up now and again from the music and gave me a quick smile. The candlelight danced over the rich wood of his violin, the beautiful Italian instrument, an old Amati, that my grandfather had given him. Kapellmeister Haydn-who was also my godfather-sat at the harpsichord, smiling broadly all the time, waving one hand in the air to direct, then putting it back on the keys and instead using his head, bobbing and nodding to bring the winds in, or to indicate the entrance for the cellos. He looked like the music, 1 remember thinking. I wish I could bring that particular sound to my mind, but it has since blended with countless, other concert. I'll always remember what it looked like, though. The light shimmering glow of everything and everyone.

Excerpt from The Musician's Daughter
by Susanne Dunlap
I thought a lot, at the beginning, about my father playing the violin. I still remember the first time I ever saw a concert. I was very small, and we had just come to Esterhaza, a magnificent palace in Sopron, Hungary, when the prince's household moved there from his winter palace, at Eisenstadt, near Vienna. I think it may have been the first concert Papa took part in there. I had a new dress for the occasion, and Mama looked beautiful. I don't think Toby was born yet, or if he was, he was a small baby and stayed in our rooms with a nurse.
The palace at EsterhÃ¡za was so new that it still smelled of raw wood and paint. The rooms were very grand. I had never seen so many candles in one place before. And they were expensive wax candles, not foul-smelling tallow. We stood to the side to watch the fine ladies enter. I thought they looked like dolls; their faces were so perfect. Later my mother told me that they painted them with white lead to make their skin artificially smooth and pale, and then rubbed rouge into their cheeks and lips, and drew lines of charcoal around their eyes so they would appear larger. And the headdresses-in those days the fashion was to have hair that towered up, stuck into place with diamond combs, with ribbons and pearls festooned around. And no matter what color it was naturally, ladies powdered their hair to snowy whiteness, so that the light of the candies was magnified by the glow from the people themselves.
And the men, too, were powdered and patched. Their gold buttons and lace were almost as gaudy as the ladies' finery. But the enormous panniers that held the ladies' skirts out to the side so far that they had to move sideways through doorways fascinated me most of all. I could barely be persuaded to turn my eyes to the orchestra when the concert started.
We were not seated with the guests. In fact, I think we were tucked away in an anteroom. The door was open so we could see through, and everyone pushed me forward to let me watch my father, who looked up now and again from the music and gave me a quick smile. The candlelight danced over the rich wood of his violin, the beautiful Italian instrument, an old Amati, that my grandfather had given him. Kapellmeister Haydn-who was also my godfather-sat at the harpsichord, smiling broadly all the time, waving one hand in the air to direct, then putting it back on the keys and instead using his head, bobbing and nodding to bring the winds in, or to indicate the entrance for the cellos. He looked like the music, 1 remember thinking. I wish I could bring that particular sound to my mind, but it has since blended with countless, other concert. I'll always remember what it looked like, though. The light shimmering glow of everything and everyone.

Question Text: Simplify 6^2 - 3^2
Rationale:
Answer: C

To simplify the expression 6^2 - 3^2, we first calculate the squares of 6 and 3, which are 36 and 9 respectively. Subtracting 9 from 36 gives us the result of 27. A) 6 This choice is incorrect because the expression involves squaring the numbers 6 and 3, not simply subtracting them directly. B) 9 This option is incorrect as it represents the square of the number 3, which is one part of the expression. However, the overall expression involves subtracting this square from the square of 6, resulting in a different value. D) 3 This option is incorrect as it represents one of the numbers in the original expression, but the operation involves squaring this number and subtracting it from the square of another number. Conclusion The correct answer is C, 27, as the expression simplifies to 6^2 - 3^2 = 36 - 9 = 27. This calculation follows the order of operations, first squaring the numbers and then subtracting the results to arrive at the final solution.

Question Text: This flow chart traces development of democratic government in England. Which event completes this sequence?

Rationale:
The relationship between an item's purchase price and each monthly payment is represented by the equation s = 12a + 50.

In this scenario, the purchase price (s dollars) is paid off in 12 equal monthly installments after a $50 down payment. Each monthly payment (a dollars) contributes to the total purchase price, with the cumulative sum reaching the initial cost plus 11 additional payments of equal value. A) s = a-50/12 This equation incorrectly subtracts $50 from each monthly payment, which would result in a diminishing total purchase price over 12 months. However, under the zero-interest financing offer, the purchase price remains constant, and the down payment is a one-time occurrence. B) s = a/12 -50 This equation divides each monthly payment by 12 and then subtracts $50, suggesting a reduction in both the purchase price and the monthly payments. Such an approach does not align with the terms of the financing offer, where the purchase price is to be paid off in equal installments. C) s = 12a + 50 The correct equation reflects the cumulative nature of the monthly payments, with each installment contributing to the total purchase price. By multiplying the monthly payment by 12 and adding the initial down payment, this equation accurately represents the relationship between the purchase price and the monthly payments. D) s = 12a - 50 In this equation, the $50 down payment is subtracted from the total purchase price, which would imply a reduction in the initial cost rather than a fixed down payment. This calculation does not adhere to the financing terms outlined in the question. E) s = 12 (a + 50) Multiplying the monthly payment by 12 and then adding $50 to the result misrepresents the relationship between the purchase price and the monthly payments. This equation implies that the down payment is multiplied by 12, which is not reflective of the given financing structure. Conclusion The correct equation s = 12a + 50 appropriately captures the relationship between an item's purchase price and the equal monthly payments required under the zero-interest financing offer. Through this equation, the cumulative effect of 12 monthly installments, along with the initial down payment, contributes to settling the total purchase price without any interest accrual.

"This, much of the baby-boom generation had well-constructed homes in which to grow up." (paragraph 4) is an opinion statement.

This statement is subjective and can be considered as an opinion because it involves a qualitative judgment or evaluation. A) "Even so, the success of Levittown depended not only on the efficiency of the Levitt brothers' process but also on the Federal Housing Administration" (paragraph 4) This statement is factual and not based on personal beliefs or feelings. B) "Shortly after World War II, many changes led to the development of suburbs." (paragraph 1) This statement is also factual. D) "With this financing in place, more homes could be built and more veterans could afford to buy them." (paragraph 4) This statement is factual. It describes the impact of a specific financing mechanism on the number of homes that could be built and the number of veterans who could afford to buy them. This is not an expression of personal belief or feeling but a statement of fact. Conclusion The statement "This, much of the baby-boom generation had well-constructed homes in which to grow up." is an opinion because it involves a subjective judgment about the quality of the homes. The other statements in the choices provide factual information about the development of Levittown, the impact of World War II on the development of suburbs, and the role of financing in making homes affordable for veterans.

Exam Name: Free GED Social Studies Practice Test
Question Text: How did Chief Justice Marshall contribute to the U.S. government system of checks and balances?
Choice A: by establishing the Supreme Court's power to check Congress
Choice B: by creating the power to remove the president from office
Choice C: by expanding the president's power to check Congress
Choice D: by giving the Supreme Court the power to control itself
Rationale:
Only Justice Murphy's dissent contended that U.S. internment camps were a clear-cut example of racial prejudice.

Justice Murphy, in his dissent, explicitly labels the internment camps as an instance of "legalization of racism," a strong condemnation of the U.S. government's actions. This contrasts with the Supreme Court's ruling, which does not characterize the internment of Japanese Americans during World War II as an act of racial prejudice. A) Only Justice Murphy's dissent acknowledged the dangers to the United States of having citizens from foreign lands. This statement is incorrect. Neither Justice Murphy's dissent nor the Supreme Court's ruling discuss the potential dangers of having citizens from foreign lands. Instead, both excerpts focus on the legality and morality of internment camps during a time of war. B) Only the Court's ruling acknowledged that the actions of fearful U.S. authorities can endanger the civil rights of citizens. This is incorrect. The Supreme Court's ruling does not explicitly acknowledge any potential endangerment to the civil rights of citizens. Instead, it justifies the actions as a necessary security measure. Conversely, Justice Murphy's dissent emphasizes the violation of civil rights and the principles of democracy. C) Only the Court's ruling contended that Korematsu was ordered held in an internment camp because he was disloyal to the United States during time of war. This is not accurate. The Court's ruling did not specifically attribute Korematsu's internment to personal disloyalty. Rather, it reasoned that he was excluded due to the overarching war circumstances and potential security risks, not explicitly because of his personal actions or loyalty. D) Only Justice Murphy's dissent contended that U.S. internment camps were a clear-cut example of racial prejudice. This is the correct answer. Justice Murphy's dissent directly and unequivocally accuses the internment camps of being an instance of legalized racism, a perspective that is not reflected in the Supreme Court's ruling. Conclusion The main difference between the U.S. Supreme Court's ruling and Justice Murphy's dissent lies in their interpretations of the internment camps. While the Supreme Court's ruling framed the internment as a security measure during war, Justice Murphy's dissent denounced it as a clear-cut example of racial prejudice, thereby underlining a distinct contrast in their respective viewpoints.

Exam Name: Free GED Social Studies Practice Test
Question Text: Which one statement identifies the main idea of President Johnson's speech?
Choice A: The federal government has the responsibility to guarantee the rights of citizens.
Choice B: The expansion of educational opportunities should be the next goal of the civil rights movement.
Choice C: The expansion of voting rights can eliminate poverty.
Choice D: The federal government has power over state governments.
Rationale:
"[O]ur people... have filled the eastern valley of the Mississippi,... ascended the Missouri to its headsprings..." is a factual statement from President James Polk's address.

This statement is based on verifiable events and occurrences. It describes the westward migration and settlement of American people, which is a historical fact. A) "Our title to... Oregon is 'clear and unquestionable,' and already are our people preparing to perfect that title by occupying it with their wives and children..." This statement is an opinion. The clarity and unquestionability of the United States' claim to Oregon was a matter of dispute, particularly in the context of the joint occupation with Britain. The phrase "clear and unquestionable" reflects Polk's perspective rather than an objective fact. C) "Nor will it become in a less degree my duty to... maintain by all constitutional means the right of the United States to that portion of our territory which lies beyond the Rocky Mountains." This statement is also an opinion. It reflects Polk's personal belief about his duty as President and the rights of the United States to the territory beyond the Rocky Mountains. These are interpretive and subjective elements, not verifiable facts. D) "The jurisdiction of our laws and the benefits of our republican institutions should be extended over them in the distant regions which they have selected for their homes." This statement is an opinion, as it expresses what Polk believes should happen—namely, the extension of U.S. jurisdiction and the benefits of republican institutions to distant regions. This is a normative claim about what ought to be done, not a description of what is factually the case. Conclusion In President James Polk's 1845 inaugural address, the only statement that can be verified as a fact rather than an opinion is the one describing the movement of American people into the eastern valley of the Mississippi and beyond. The other statements reflect Polk's personal beliefs and interpretations about the United States' rights to territories, his own duties, and what ought to be done, making them subjective and interpretive rather than objective and factual.

The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) was created in 1909 by African Americans and others to ensure the political, educational, social, and economic equality of rights of all persons and to eliminate race-based discrimination. This was a clear demonstration of African Americans taking action to defend their civil rights. B) the issuance of the Emancipation Proclamation While the Emancipation Proclamation was a landmark event in the fight for civil rights, it was not an action taken by African Americans to defend their rights. Rather, it was a decree issued by President Abraham Lincoln during the Civil War. C) the start of the Great Migration The Great Migration was a mass movement of African Americans from the rural South to the urban North in search of better economic opportunities and to escape racial segregation. Although this event may show African Americans taking action for better living conditions, it does not directly indicate a defense of their civil rights. Conclusion The founding of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People is the only option that directly indicates African Americans actively defending their civil rights. The other choices, while significant in the context of civil rights history, were not direct actions taken by African Americans to defend their rights or were not directly related to the defense of civil rights. Hence, the NAACP's creation counters the newspaper excerpt's viewpoint that African Americans refused to defend their civil rights.

Exam Name: Free GED Social Studies Practice Test
Question Text: How does the message presented by Senator Paul differ from the message presented by President Obama?
Choice A: Only Senator Paul promotes less government involvement in health care.
Choice B: Only Senator Paul wants to secure more health care for U.S. citizens.
Choice C: Only President Obama supports the Supreme Court's right to rule about health care.
Choice D: Only President Obama discusses problems in the health care industry.
Rationale:
Baron de Montesquieu would most likely support the presidential power to appoint federal judges with the consent of the U.S. Senate.

Baron de Montesquieu advocated for a system of government with separation of powers where different branches keep each other in check. The ability of a president to appoint federal judges, but only with the consent of the Senate, fits well within this philosophy. A) Thomas Hobbes Thomas Hobbes primarily advocated for a strong, centralized authority in the form of a monarchy. He believed that such a system was necessary to prevent a state of nature where life is "nasty, brutish, and short." The system of presidential appointment of federal judges with Senate approval involves checks and balances, a concept that doesn't align with Hobbes's preference for centralized authority. B) Baron de Montesquieu Baron de Montesquieu is known for his advocacy of the separation of powers in government. This concept involves dividing governmental responsibilities into distinct branches to limit any one branch from exercising the core functions of another. The system of presidential appointment of federal judges with the consent of the Senate is a perfect example of this separation and interplay of powers, as it involves both the executive and legislative branches of government. C) Jean-Jacques Rousseau Jean-Jacques Rousseau is known for his social contract theory wherein the government is a contract between rulers and the people. Though he believed in democracy and the general will of the people, the specific system of checks and balances involved in the appointment of federal judges may not directly align with his philosophy. D) John Locke John Locke was an influential enlightenment thinker known for his views on government's role in protecting life, liberty, and property. While he believed in a system of government where different branches have distinct responsibilities, his philosophy doesn't specifically advocate for a system where the executive branch appoints federal judges with legislative branch approval. Conclusion Based on the philosophies of these thinkers, Baron de Montesquieu, who advocated for the separation of powers in government, would most likely support the presidential power to appoint federal judges with the consent of the U.S. Senate. This system represents a clear example of the checks and balances that Montesquieu believed were essential to a functioning and fair government.

Throughout the address, Johnson emphasizes the federal government's duty to protect and uphold the rights of its citizens, particularly focusing on the right to vote. He advocates for the passage of a bill to address voting rights and affirms the struggle for human rights as a national concern. B) The expansion of educational opportunities should be the next goal of the civil rights movement. While Johnson mentions education in his speech, he does not position it as the next specific goal of the civil rights movement. His emphasis is primarily on the protection of voting rights and the role of the federal government in ensuring these rights, making this choice inaccurate. C) The expansion of voting rights can eliminate poverty. President Johnson does mention poverty as one of the enemies to overcome, but he does not make a direct connection between the expansion of voting rights and the elimination of poverty. This choice misrepresents the main idea of Johnson's speech. D) The federal government has power over state governments. Although Johnson's speech implies the federal government's authority in protecting citizens' rights, he does not explicitly state that the federal government has power over state governments. His focus is more on the responsibility of the federal government towards its citizens rather than its power over states. Conclusion The main idea of President Johnson's speech is the responsibility of the federal government to guarantee and protect the rights of its citizens, especially the right to vote. While other issues such as education, poverty, and the relationship between federal and state governments are mentioned, they do not represent the central theme of Johnson's address. The speech primarily advocates for the protection of citizens' rights as a crucial duty of the federal government.

They are utilizing a 1st Amendment right to secure equality under the law.

The 1st Amendment of the United States Constitution protects several basic liberties including freedom of religion, speech, press, peaceable assembly, and the right to petition the government. As such, the people in the photograph could be exercising their 1st Amendment rights if they are speaking out, protesting, or petitioning the government to secure equality under the law. A) They are utilizing an 8th Amendment right to secure the release of a colleague who was jailed unfairly. The 8th Amendment primarily protects individuals from excessive bail, excessive fines, and cruel and unusual punishments. It does not specifically provide a right to secure the release of someone who was jailed unfairly. The action of securing release would more directly relate to legal processes or possibly the right to a fair trial under the 6th Amendment. B) They are utilizing a 6th Amendment right to secure a fair trial for a jailed associate. The 6th Amendment provides the right to a speedy and public trial, the right to an impartial jury, the right to be informed of the charges, the right to confront witnesses, the right to compulsory process for obtaining witnesses, and the right to counsel. While it could be related to securing a fair trial for a jailed associate, this choice does not necessarily describe the action of people in the photograph if they are not directly involved in a trial process. D) They are utilizing a 14th Amendment right to secure the opportunity to vote in federal elections. The 14th Amendment primarily addresses citizenship rights and equal protection of the laws. While it has been used to protect voting rights, it does not specifically provide a right to secure the opportunity to vote in federal elections. This would be more directly related to the 15th, 19th, or 26th Amendments which explicitly protect the right to vote. Conclusion The people in the photograph are most accurately described as exercising a right protected by the 1st Amendment, which includes the freedom of speech, press, peaceable assembly, and petitioning the government. While these rights could potentially be used in relation to issues covered by the other amendments mentioned, the specific actions described in the other choices do not match the rights explicitly protected by those amendments. Therefore, the most fitting answer is that they are using a 1st Amendment right to secure equality under the law.

Exam Name: Free GED Social Studies Practice Test
Question Text: Which statement is supported by the diagrams?
Choice A: Only the vice president and the president pro tempore of the Senate serve in two branches.
Choice B: Only officials from the executive and legislative branches are in the line of presidential succession.
Choice C: The order of presidential succession is determined by the members of the executive branch.
Choice D: The leaders of Congress are accountable to the members of the executive branch.
Rationale:
Chief Justice Marshall contributed to the U.S. government system of checks and balances by establishing the Supreme Court's power to check Congress.

Through the Marbury v. Madison case, Chief Justice Marshall asserted the authority of the Supreme Court to declare laws passed by Congress as unconstitutional, thereby providing a check on the legislative branch. This power, known as judicial review, is not explicitly mentioned in the Constitution but has become a fundamental part of the U.S. system of government. A) by establishing the Supreme Court's power to check Congress This is the correct answer as the excerpt clearly states that Chief Justice Marshall, through the Marbury v. Madison case, established the power of the Supreme Court to declare a law unconstitutional. This marked the first time the Court exercised this power, thereby providing a check on the powers of Congress. B) by creating the power to remove the president from office This statement is incorrect. The power to remove the president from office, or impeachment, is vested in Congress, not the Supreme Court. The Constitution grants the House of Representatives the power to impeach, and the Senate the power to try impeachments. C) by expanding the president's power to check Congress This statement is incorrect. Chief Justice Marshall did not expand the president's power to check Congress. The Constitution provides the president with the power to veto laws passed by Congress, but this veto power is not related to the actions of Chief Justice Marshall. D) by giving the Supreme Court the power to control itself This statement is incorrect. While it can be argued that the establishment of judicial review gave the Supreme Court a certain degree of self-regulation, the phrase "control itself" is misleading. The primary impact of Marshall's actions was to check the power of Congress, not to control the operations of the Supreme Court itself. Conclusion Chief Justice Marshall's establishment of the Supreme Court's power of judicial review served as a significant contribution to the U.S. government system of checks and balances. Through this action, Marshall asserted the Court's role in checking the legislative power of Congress, thereby strengthening the balance of powers among the three branches of government.

Exam Name: Free GED Social Studies Practice Test
Question Text: What basic assumption behind Marshall's remarks is not behind Reagan's remarks?
Choice A: The framers of the U.S. Constitution have received more credit than they deserve for the governmental protections Americans enjoy.
Choice B: The framers of the U.S. Constitution have been credited with creating a document that protects all free people around the world.
Choice C: The U.S. Constitution created a government whose promises are yet to be realized by many around the world.
Choice D: The U.S. Constitution created a government based on the principles expressed in the Declaration of Independence.
Rationale:
The branches of government are more independent of each other in a presidential democracy than in a parliamentary democracy.

In a presidential democracy, the executive, legislative, and judicial branches are clearly separate entities that operate independently of each other. This separation of powers is designed to prevent any one branch from holding too much power, with each branch having its own distinct responsibilities. A) The legislature is more representative of the people in a presidential democracy than in a parliamentary democracy. This statement is not necessarily true as representation in the legislature largely depends on the electoral system and not the form of democracy. Both presidential and parliamentary democracies can have highly representative legislatures, depending on the specific mechanisms in place for electing representatives. B) The branches of government are more independent of each other in a presidential democracy than in a parliamentary democracy. This statement is accurate. In a presidential democracy, the three branches of government are separate and independent from each other, with checks and balances in place to prevent the concentration of power. Conversely, in a parliamentary democracy, the executive branch is typically formed by the party or coalition that holds the majority in the legislature, showing a close interdependence between these two branches. C) The executive branch is more accountable to the people in a parliamentary democracy than in a direct democracy. This statement is not correct. In a parliamentary system, the executive branch is accountable to the parliament, which in turn represents the people. In a direct democracy, the people themselves make decisions and the executive branch is directly accountable to them. Therefore, accountability would theoretically be higher in a direct democracy. D) The people are more involved in governing in a presidential democracy than in a direct democracy. This statement is not correct. In a direct democracy, the people themselves make all governing decisions, which is the highest level of involvement possible. In a presidential democracy, the people elect representatives to make decisions on their behalf, which is a lower level of direct involvement in governing. Conclusion The diagrams suggest that the branches of government are more independent of each other in a presidential democracy than in a parliamentary democracy. This independence is a key feature of the separation of powers in a presidential system. The other options are not conclusively supported by the diagrams, as they involve aspects of representation, accountability, and citizen involvement that depend on specific circumstances and mechanisms, and not solely on the type of democracy.

Exam Name: Free GED Social Studies Practice Test
Question Text: Which event counters the viewpoint expressed in the newspaper excerpt that African Americans refused to defend their civil rights?

Choice C: the founding of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People
Choice D: the start of the Great Migration
Rationale:
President Nixon's message was intended to convince Congress that it must participate in actions to address human damage to the environment.

In his message presented to the U.S. Congress in 1970, President Nixon underlines the need for new philosophies regarding the utilization of land, air, and water. He emphasizes the need for stricter regulation, expanded government action, and increased citizen involvement. He urges for the implementation of new programs that ensure everyone, including the government, industry, and individuals, contribute to the mitigation of environmental damage and share the costs. A) to convince Congress that technological advancement and population growth must be slowed to protect the environment While President Nixon acknowledges that environmental damage is not an inevitable by-product of advancing technology or growing population, he does not suggest that technological advancement and population growth should be slowed down. His focus is more on better regulation and responsibility sharing, rather than slowing down progress. B) to convince Congress that industrial polluters of the air and water should not pay for cleaning the environment This is incorrect because President Nixon explicitly states that new programs should ensure that the government, industry, and individuals all pay their fair share of the cost to address environmental concerns. He does not advocate for exempting industrial polluters from the responsibility of environmental cleanup. C) to convince Congress that it must take part in action to address human damage to the environment This is the correct answer. President Nixon states that the tasks that need doing call for expanded government action, indicating that he wants Congress to take part in addressing the environmental damage caused by human activities. D) to convince Congress that there should be no benefits for businesses that pollute the environment President Nixon does not mention anything about withdrawing benefits from businesses that pollute the environment in his message. His focus is on creating new programs that ensure all stakeholders, including businesses, participate in mitigating environmental damage and share the costs. Conclusion President Nixon's message to the U.S. Congress in 1970 emphasized the need for an inclusive approach to address environmental damage. He advocated for stricter regulation, broader government action, greater citizen involvement, and the creation of new programs that ensure all parties, including the government, industry, and individuals, contribute and share the costs. While he acknowledged the role of advancing technology and growing population, he did not suggest slowing them down, nor did he propose exempting businesses from the responsibilities of environmental cleanup.

Exam Name: Free GED Social Studies Practice Test
Question Text: Based on the excerpt, which change was a result of the National Security Act of 1947?
Choice A: The Central Intelligence Agency replaced the War Department.
Choice B: The National Security Council gained control over the Department of Defense.
Choice C: The Department of Homeland Security replaced the War Department.
Choice D: The Department of the Navy became part of the Department of Defense.
Rationale:
Government spending increased due to national defense.

The heightened government spending during World War II for national defense and military operations significantly contributed to the peak annual inflation rate in the 1940s. This was because the massive procurement of arms and equipment led to an increase in overall demand, which consequently pushed prices higher. A) Consumer demand increased due to the end of rationing. This statement is incorrect because it pertains to the period after World War II. Rationing was a common practice during the war to ensure the availability of essential goods for all citizens. Only when the war ended and rationing ceased did consumer demand increase drastically, contributing to inflation. However, this surge in demand occurred primarily in the late 1940s, after the period of peak inflation. B) Manufacturing decreased because of less demand for weapons. This statement is also incorrect as it refers to the period after the war. During World War II, manufacturing actually increased due to the high demand for weapons and other war materials. The decrease in manufacturing and corresponding drop in demand for weapons occurred after the war ended in 1945, which would have had a deflationary effect rather than contributing to inflation. C) Wages decreased because of competition for scarce jobs. This statement is incorrect. During World War II, the United States saw a decrease in unemployment and an increase in wages due to the high demand for labor in war production. This in turn led to an increase in purchasing power among workers, which could contribute to inflation. However, the statement falsely asserts that wages decreased due to competition for jobs, which was not the case during the war years. Conclusion The peak annual inflation rate during the 1940s in the United States was primarily caused by an increase in government spending for national defense during World War II. This period saw a surge in demand for goods due to the war effort, which led to higher prices. The other options, referring to the end of rationing, decreased manufacturing, and decreased wages, describe conditions that occurred after the war and thus do not explain the peak inflation during the 1940s.

Question Text: What is the meaning of confederation in this passage?

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test

The political party's presidential candidate is selected by votes cast in party primary elections.

Primary elections are a way for political parties to narrow down the field of candidates in the lead-up to the general election. They are held before the party convention and allow party members to vote for their preferred candidate. The candidate with the most votes usually becomes the party's nominee for the presidential election. A) by balloting after the general election This choice is incorrect because the general election is the final stage in the election process, where the chosen candidates of each party go head-to-head. The selection of a party's presidential candidate must occur before the general election, not after. B) by balloting at the party convention This choice is not accurate because, while the party convention is an important event, it is typically used to announce and rally support for the already selected party candidate. The actual selection of the candidate is determined by the party's primary elections. C) by votes cast in party primary elections This is the correct choice. Party primary elections are held to determine the party's presidential candidate. Party members vote for their preferred candidate in these elections, and the candidate with the most votes typically becomes the party's nominee. D) by votes cast in the general election This choice is incorrect because the general election is the final stage in the election process. At this point, each party's candidate has already been selected and is competing against the candidates of other parties for the presidency. Conclusion In the process of selecting a political party's presidential candidate, the key step is the party primary elections. During these elections, party members vote for their preferred candidate. The candidate with the most votes usually becomes the party's nominee. The general election, which is the final stage of the election process, is a competition between the selected candidates of all parties, rather than a stage for selecting a party's candidate.

It does not align with the passage, which explains that under the Articles of Confederation, the states, not the population, held the majority of the political authority. B) a political union in which power is divided between a strong central authority and the various other political units This choice is incorrect because it describes a federal system, not a confederation. In the passage, it is clear that the central authority (the confederation government) was not strong and the states retained their "sovereignty, freedom, and independence". C) a political union in which the component units retain significant independence from the central government This choice is accurate as it is consistent with the passage. The Articles of Confederation established a "firm league of friendship" between the states and reserved the greatest share of political authority to the individual states, which signifies that the component units (the states) retained significant independence. D) a government in which people vote to elect representatives who make laws for the people This choice describes a representative democracy and is not the definition of a confederation as outlined in the passage. In the described confederation, the power was held mainly by the states, not the elected representatives of the people. Conclusion In the context of the passage, a confederation is a political union where the component units retain significant independence from the central government. This is demonstrated by the 1781 confederation of the 13 former British American colonies, in which the states retained a high degree of "sovereignty, freedom, and independence". The other options, which describe different forms of government such as direct democracy, federalism, and representative democracy, do not align with the definition provided in the passage.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: Which statement from the passage is an opinion?
Choice A: "Even so, the success of Levittown depended not only on the efficiency of the Levitt brothers' process but also on the Federal Housing Administration" (paragraph 4)
Choice B: "Shortly after World War II, many changes led to the development of suburbs." (paragraph 1)
Choice C: "This, much of the baby-boom generation had well-constructed homes in which to grow up." (paragraph 4)
Choice D: "With this financing in place, more homes could be built and more veterans could afford to buy them." (paragraph 4)
Rationale:
Ratification of the 19th Amendment was the result of the accumulated efforts of women for decades.

The passage describes the long journey towards the ratification of the 19th Amendment, which granted women the right to vote. This journey began in the colonial era and involved various efforts, including advocating for gender equality in political and civil rights (as seen at the Seneca Falls Convention) and supporting the war effort during World War I. A) Ratification was the result of the Seneca Falls Convention. While the Seneca Falls Convention was a significant event in the struggle for women's suffrage, it was not the sole cause for the ratification of the 19th Amendment. The convention, held in 1848, was a part of the broader movement for women's rights, which included efforts from the colonial era to the aftermath of World War I. B) Ratification occurred in return for the military service of women during World War I. The passage does mention that women's work to support the war effort during World War I was greatly admired. However, it does not state that the ratification of the 19th Amendment was a direct result of women's military service during the war. Therefore, this option is not supported by the passage. C) Ratification occurred prior to the granting of voting rights to freed slaves. This statement is not accurate according to the passage. The passage mentions that the ratification of the Civil War Amendments, which granted voting rights to freed slaves, occurred before the struggle for women's suffrage resulted in the ratification of the 19th Amendment. D) Ratification was the result of the accumulated efforts of women for decades. This statement is consistent with the information provided in the passage. The passage outlines a timeline of women's efforts for suffrage that spanned multiple decades, from the colonial era to the 1920s, when the 19th Amendment was finally ratified. Conclusion Based on the passage, the ratification of the 19th Amendment was the culmination of women's efforts over many decades, starting from the colonial era and continuing through significant events like the Seneca Falls Convention and World War I. The other options either overstate the impact of one specific event or are not supported by the timeline provided in the passage. Therefore, the correct answer is that the ratification was the result of the accumulated efforts of women for decades.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: Which event occurred first?
Choice A: Michael moved to Ohio.
Choice B: Michael attained U.S. citizenship.
Choice C: The IRO was created.
Choice D: The UN was established.
Rationale:
Individuals sell their labor in factor markets.

The text clearly mentions that the factors of production (land, labor, capital, and entrepreneurship) are traded in the factor market. This means that individuals provide their labor in exchange for wages or income in the factor market. A) Individuals spend their income in factor markets. This statement is incorrect as per the text. The text mentions that individuals spend the money they have earned in the factor market, not in it, but to make purchases in product markets. Therefore, individuals do not spend their income in factor markets. B) Entrepreneurs profit from participation in product markets. While entrepreneurs might indeed profit from participating in product markets, this information is not stated or implied in the given text. The text describes the factor market and product market, but does not make specific claims about entrepreneurs profiting from product markets. C) Individuals sell their labor in factor markets. This statement is correct. According to the text, labor, as a factor of production, is bought and sold in the factor market. This means that individuals provide their labor in exchange for income in the factor market. D) Resources are traded in product markets. This statement is incorrect according to the text. Resources or factors of production, such as land, labor, and capital, are traded in the factor market, not in the product market. The product market is where producers offer goods and services for sale. Conclusion Based on the text, we can conclude that individuals sell their labor in the factor market and not in the product market. The text does not mention that individuals spend their income in factor markets, entrepreneurs profit from participation in product markets, or resources are traded in product markets. Therefore, the statement 'Individuals sell their labor in factor markets' is the only one that accurately represents the information provided in the text.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: What impact do the varying insurance rates of the Revenue Protection Plan have on farming?
Choice A: They maintain stable prices for certain crops.
Choice B: They give farmers an incentive to promote crop diversity.
Choice C: They ensure the steady increase of crop production levels.
Choice D: They give farmers an incentive to grow crops that have higher subsidies.
Rationale:
Ratification of the Constitution was dependent on the passage of the Bill of Rights.

The excerpt clearly states that the Constitution's ratification was contingent upon the immediate addition of amendments protecting individual rights. These amendments collectively became known as the Bill of Rights. This indicates that the commitment to add a Bill of Rights was a critical factor in gaining the necessary support for ratifying the Constitution. A) Ratification of the Bill of Rights was completed before the Constitution was drafted. This choice is incorrect because the excerpt explicitly states that calls for amendments protecting individual rights came after the Constitution was drafted and during the first Congress in September 1789. The amendments, which became known as the Bill of Rights, were ratified by December 1791, clearly after the Constitution's drafting. B) Ratification of the Constitution was dependent on the passage of the Bill of Rights. This is the correct answer. The excerpt clearly shows that five states, including Massachusetts, agreed to ratify the Constitution only on the condition that amendments protecting individual rights would be added immediately. The subsequent ratification of these amendments, which became the Bill of Rights, fulfilled this condition. C) Most leaders were worried that the Bill of Rights would weaken the ruling class protected by the Constitution. The excerpt does not provide any evidence that leaders were concerned about the Bill of Rights weakening the ruling class. Instead, it indicates a near-unanimous agreement on the need for amendments to protect individual rights. D) Many leaders were concerned that the Bill of Rights would upset the balance of powers outlined in the Constitution. The excerpt does not suggest any concern about the Bill of Rights upsetting the balance of powers. The focus was rather on ensuring the protection of individual rights, which led to the drafting and ratification of the Bill of Rights. Conclusion The Constitutional Convention led to the drafting of the U.S. Constitution, but the ratification by several states was made contingent upon the addition of amendments protecting individual rights. These amendments, known collectively as the Bill of Rights, were ratified by December 1791, indicating that the adoption of the Constitution was indeed dependent on the passage of the Bill of Rights.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: Which statement from Common Sense provides evidence that the author Thomas Paine supported American independence?
Choice A: "Absolute governments have this advantage with them, that they are simple..."
Choice B: "I challenge the warmest advocate for reconciliation, to [show], a single advantage that this continent can reap, by being connected with Great Britain."
Choice C: "The prejudice of Englishmen, in [favor] of their own government arises as much or more from national pride than reason."
Choice D: "Society in every state is a blessing, but government even in its best state is but a necessary evil."
Rationale:
Only Senator Paul promotes less government involvement in health care.

In the provided excerpts, Senator Paul is mainly advocating for less government intervention in health care, arguing for serious market reforms and more freedom. This contrasts with President Obama's position, which supports the Affordable Care Act—a legislation that necessitates a certain level of government involvement in health care. A) Only Senator Paul promotes less government involvement in health care. This is correct. Excerpts from Senator Paul's article argue for less government involvement, suggesting that the health care industry is overregulated and that solutions should offer more freedom. On the other hand, President Obama's excerpts show his support for the Affordable Care Act, which necessitates governmental involvement in health care. B) Only Senator Paul wants to secure more health care for U.S. citizens. This is incorrect. Neither Senator Paul nor President Obama's excerpts suggest that only Senator Paul wants to secure more health care for U.S. citizens. While Senator Paul does argue for market reforms in the health care industry, he does not explicitly state that this would result in more health care for U.S. citizens. President Obama, on the other hand, suggests that the Affordable Care Act will make lives more secure, implying an increase in health care accessibility. C) Only President Obama supports the Supreme Court's right to rule about health care. This is incorrect. The provided excerpts do not contain any information regarding either Senator Paul's or President Obama's views on the Supreme Court's right to rule about health care. Although President Obama does express satisfaction with the Supreme Court's ruling on the Affordable Care Act, this does not necessarily mean that only he supports the court's right to rule on health care matters. D) Only President Obama discusses problems in the health care industry. This is incorrect. Both Senator Paul and President Obama discuss problems in the health care industry in their respective excerpts. Senator Paul suggests that the industry is overregulated and promotes market reforms, while President Obama discusses the provision in the Affordable Care Act that requires people who can afford health insurance to buy it, implying a problem of uninsured citizens. Conclusion The main difference between Senator Paul's and President Obama's positions, as presented in their respective excerpts, is their view on government involvement in health care. Senator Paul argues for less government intervention, while President Obama supports the Affordable Care Act, which involves government intervention in the health care industry. The other options are either not supported by the provided excerpts or are applicable to both individuals.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: Assuming that the majority of senators and representatives voted along party lines, which conclusion about the U.S. government in 2011 does the table support?
Choice A: The party that passed more legislation could be anticipated.
Choice B: The party that would win the next presidential election could be predicted.
Choice C: Party divisions were a challenge to the legislative process.
Choice D: Party affiliations are a requirement for congressional membership.
Rationale:
Only officials from the executive and legislative branches are in the line of presidential succession.

The line of presidential succession refers to the specific order in which officials are designated to assume the presidency in the event that the incumbent is unable to fulfill their duties. This hierarchy is outlined in the U.S. Constitution and is composed of officials from both the executive and legislative branches. A) Only the vice president and the president pro tempore of the Senate serve in two branches This statement is incorrect. The vice president and the president pro tempore of the Senate do not serve in two branches. The vice president, although being the president of the Senate, is part of the executive branch. The president pro tempore of the Senate is part of the legislative branch. B) Only officials from the executive and legislative branches are in the line of presidential succession This is the correct answer. In the line of presidential succession, the vice president is first, followed by the speaker of the House, and then the president pro tempore of the Senate. These positions are followed by various cabinet positions, all of which belong to either the executive or legislative branches of government. C) The order of presidential succession is determined by the members of the executive branch This is incorrect. The order of presidential succession is determined by the U.S. Constitution, not by the members of the executive branch. It is a predefined sequence that cannot be altered by current officeholders. D) The leaders of Congress are accountable to the members of the executive branch This statement is inaccurate. The leaders of Congress are directly accountable to their constituents, not to members of the executive branch. The principle of separation of powers provides for checks and balances between the different branches, but does not make one branch accountable to another. Conclusion The line of presidential succession is a constitutional provision that outlines the sequence in which officials from the executive and legislative branches would assume the presidency if the incumbent is unable to serve. This line does not include members from the judiciary branch, nor is it determined by current members of the executive branch. Also, the leaders of Congress are not accountable to the executive branch, but to their constituents. Therefore, only the statement "Only officials from the executive and legislative branches are in the line of presidential succession" is supported by the diagrams.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: Which quotation from the excerpt reveals the author's bias about the relationship between the rule of the majority and the rights of the minority?

Choice B: "Requiring a unanimous vote or more than a majority vote is meant to provide a safeguard against oppression."

Only Senator Russell said that state governments were sufficiently protecting the rights of citizens.

Senator Russell's position, indicated in the incomplete telegram, is implied to support the idea that state governments were doing an adequate job in protecting citizen rights. On the other hand, President Johnson's speech clearly calls for federal intervention to safeguard the rights of all Americans, particularly their right to vote. A) Only Senator Russell said that state governments were sufficiently protecting the rights of citizens. While the excerpt from Senator Russell's telegram does not explicitly state this, the implication is that he believes state governments are adequately handling citizen rights. This is in contrast to President Johnson, who explicitly calls for federal intervention to protect citizens' rights, particularly their voting rights. B) Only Senator Russell supported the federal government's intervention. This is incorrect as Senator Russell's telegram is protesting against 'highhanded and illegal methods', which suggests a disagreement with the federal government's intervention. On the other hand, President Johnson's speech calls for federal intervention to protect citizens' rights. It's not indicated in the excerpts whether Senator Russell supports this position. D) Only President Johnson believed that the federal government was authorized to intervene. Conclusion The key difference between the positions expressed by President Johnson and Senator Russell lies in their views on the sufficiency of state governments in protecting the rights of citizens. President Johnson clearly calls for federal intervention, indicating a belief that state governments are not adequately protecting these rights. Conversely, Senator Russell's telegram implies a belief that state governments are sufficiently handling the task, as he protests against 'highhanded and illegal methods', likely referring to federal intervention.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: Which statement provides the main idea of the passage?
Choice A: The founders understood the importance of dividing war powers in the U.S. Constitution.
Choice B: President Truman used presidential war powers to send troops to South Korea.

"In these circumstances, expect constant scheming, trickery, and tiresome delays, resulting in disgraceful deals that violate the public good."

This statement implies the author's negative bias towards a rule that empowers the minority to block the majority's decisions. The author views this rule as a catalyst for unscrupulous behavior and harmful outcomes for the public good, which reveals his bias about the relationship between the rule of the majority and the rights of the minority. A) "The wishes of the smaller group will override the wishes of the larger group and this will create resentment." B) "Requiring a unanimous vote or more than a majority vote is meant to provide a safeguard against oppression." This statement represents a common argument for requiring more than a majority vote—it is meant to protect against oppression. However, it does not directly reveal the author's personal bias, as it merely describes the intended purpose of the rule without expressing a judgment about its effectiveness or consequences. D) "In times of national emergency, the goodness, badness, weakness, and strength of the government is supremely important." This statement emphasizes the importance of the government's character and performance during national emergencies. However, it does not reveal the author's bias about the relationship between the rule of the majority and the rights of the minority. It is a general statement about government responsibility, not a judgment about the specific rule in question. Conclusion The author's bias about the relationship between the rule of the majority and the rights of the minority is most clearly revealed in the statement, "In these circumstances, expect constant scheming, trickery, and tiresome delays, resulting in disgraceful deals that violate the public good." This statement portrays the author’s negative perspective towards the rule that empowers a minority to block the majority's decisions, as he perceives it as a source of detrimental outcomes for the public good. The other options either describe possible outcomes or purposes of the rule without revealing personal judgement or bias.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test

Rationale:
(C) (3,3)

The correct answer corresponds to the vertex of the parabola formed by the given quadratic equation. The vertex of a parabola in the form y = ax² + bx + c can be found using the formula x = -b / 2a. By substituting the coefficients from the equation y = -3x² + 18x - 24, you can determine the x-coordinate of the vertex, which is 3. Substituting this x-value back into the original equation provides the corresponding y-coordinate of 3, confirming the vertex as (3, 3). A) (6, -24) This point does not represent the vertex of the parabola. The x-coordinate is incorrect, as the correct x-value for the vertex is 3, not 6. Additionally, the y-value of -24 does not align with the calculated y-coordinate of 3 for the vertex. B) (4, 0) The coordinates provided do not match the vertex of the parabola. The x-value is incorrect, as the vertex x-coordinate should be 3 rather than 4. Moreover, the y-value of 0 does not correspond to the calculated y-coordinate of 3 for the vertex. D) (2, 0) These coordinates are not indicative of the vertex of the parabola represented by the given equation. The x-value is inaccurately given as 2, whereas the correct x-coordinate for the vertex is 3. Additionally, the y-coordinate provided as 0 does not align with the calculated y-coordinate of 3 for the vertex. E) (-3, -105) The coordinates (-3, -105) do not represent the vertex of the parabola. The x-value of -3 is incorrect, as the vertex x-coordinate should be 3, not -3. Furthermore, the y-coordinate of -105 does not match the calculated y-coordinate of 3 for the vertex. Conclusion The vertex of a parabola is a crucial point that signifies the maximum or minimum value of the quadratic function. In this case, the correct vertex coordinates for the parabola represented by the equation y = -3x² + 18x - 24 are (3, 3). These coordinates mark the turning point of the parabolic curve, where the function reaches its extreme value.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: Which statement from President Polk's address is a fact rather than an opinion?
Choice A: "Our title to Oregon is 'clear and unquestionable,' and already are our people preparing to perfect that title by occupying it with their wives and children..."
Choice B: "[O]ur people have filled the eastern valley of the Mississippi,... ascended the Missouri to its headsprings..."
Choice C: "Nor will it become in a less degree my duty to maintain by all constitutional means the right of the United States to that portion of our territory which lies beyond the Rocky Mountains."
Choice D: "The jurisdiction of our laws and the benefits of our republican institutions should be extended over them in the distant regions which they have selected for their homes."
Rationale:
The United States' involvement in World War I most likely shaped the author's point of view.

The extract from the book "Essentials of Americanization" by Emory S. Bogardus discusses the Americanization movement's origins in 1914, a year marking the onset of World War I. His references to nationalism, autocracy, and world domination suggest a response to the geopolitical environment of this period, when several powerful nations were embroiled in a major conflict. A) the development of the concept of Manifest Destiny Manifest Destiny, a 19th-century doctrine advocating westward expansion by the United States, is not directly referenced in the extract. The author's focus on nationalism, imperialism, and democracy more closely aligns with the global issues surrounding World War I rather than the specific expansionist agenda of the Manifest Destiny era. B) the United States' involvement in World War I The author's discussion of a resurgence in nationalism aligns with the period of World War I, when many nations, including the United States, experienced heightened nationalistic sentiments. His warnings against autocracy, imperialism, and ambition for world domination also reflect common criticisms of the Central Powers during this war. This context, coupled with the specific mention of 1914 as the Americanization movement's origin, strongly suggests that the author's viewpoint was shaped by World War I. C) the United States' internal conflicts during the Great Depression While the Great Depression indeed prompted significant societal introspection and policy shifts within the United States, the extract does not indicate a focus on economic struggles or internal conflicts characteristic of this period. The discussions of nationalism, autocracy, and world domination are far more indicative of a World War I context. D) the calls for social reform during Prohibition Prohibition, a period of U.S. history characterized by the ban on alcohol, spurred many social reforms. However, the author's focus on nationalistic principles, imperialism, and the need for a "perfected democracy" appear unconnected to the specific issues addressed during Prohibition, making it an unlikely influence on his viewpoint. Conclusion The time frame and thematic content of Emory S. Bogardus's statements strongly indicate that his views on the Americanization movement were shaped by the United States' involvement in World War I. The rise of nationalism, the struggle against autocracy and imperialism, as well as the call for a perfected democracy, all reflect key aspects of the geopolitical landscape during this period. Other historical events, such as the Manifest Destiny, the Great Depression, and Prohibition, do not align as closely with the author's expressed perspectives.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: Which statement identifies the purpose of President Nixon's message?
Choice A: to convince Congress that technological advancement and population growth must be slowed to protect the environment
Choice B: to convince Congress that industrial polluters of the air and water should not pay for cleaning the environment
Choice C: to convince Congress that it must take part in action to address human damage to the environment
Choice D: to convince Congress that there should be no benefits for businesses that pollute the environment
Rationale:
"I challenge the warmest advocate for reconciliation, to [show], a single advantage that this continent can reap, by being connected with Great Britain."

This statement by Thomas Paine in Common Sense directly challenges proponents of reconciliation with Great Britain, implying that he sees no benefits in maintaining a political connection. His provocative challenge underscores his support for American independence. A) "Absolute governments have this advantage with them, that they are simple..." This quote does not provide evidence of Paine's support for American independence. Rather, it discusses the nature of absolute governments, without directly addressing the relationship between America and Great Britain or advocating for independence. B) "I challenge the warmest advocate for reconciliation, to [show], a single advantage that this continent can reap, by being connected with Great Britain." This quote directly challenges the benefits of America's political connection with Great Britain. Paine is asking advocates for reconciliation to demonstrate a single advantage that America could gain from this relationship. His challenge indicates a strong support for American independence, as he implies that no such advantages exist. C) "The prejudice of Englishmen, in [favor] of their own government arises as much or more from national pride than reason." While this statement criticizes Englishmen's favoritism toward their own government, it does not directly imply Paine's support for American independence. It more so critiques the basis of Englishmen's political preferences, rather than advocating for a political separation between America and Great Britain. D) "Society in every state is a blessing, but government even in its best state is but a necessary evil." This quote reflects Paine's general view on government, but it does not provide evidence of his support for American independence. His statement does not specifically address the political situation between America and Great Britain. Conclusion The statement that best evidences Thomas Paine's support for American independence is his challenge to proponents of reconciliation to show a single benefit that America could gain by maintaining a political connection with Great Britain. His direct challenge, coupled with the implication that no such benefits exist, strongly indicates his support for American independence. The other statements, while reflective of Paine's views on government, do not directly advocate for or provide evidence of his support for independence.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: Which statement best describes the action of the people in the photograph?
Choice A: They are utilizing an 8th Amendment right to secure the release of a colleague who was jailed unfairly.
Choice B: They are utilizing a 6th Amendment right to secure a fair trial for a jailed associate.
Choice C: They are utilizing a 1st Amendment right to secure equality under the law.
Choice D: They are utilizing a 14th Amendment right to secure the opportunity to vote in federal elections.
Rationale:
The majority is able to achieve special rights.

In the excerpts from Alexis de Tocqueville's "Democracy in America," he argues that a potential source of conflict in the U.S. system of government is that the majority can acquire exclusive privileges. This could lead to a misuse of power, as they might act against the interests of the minority. A) The majority is able to achieve special rights. In both excerpts, de Tocqueville indicates that the majority has the potential to misuse its power by wronging the minority, creating conflict. He suggests that the majority may seek to deprive a minority of their rights or privileges, which can result in societal unrest. B) The minority is able to block popular legislation. De Tocqueville doesn't discuss the ability of the minority to block popular legislation in these excerpts. His focus is on the potential for the majority to misuse its power and cause conflict, not on the minority's potential resistance to legislation. C) Legislators must represent minority interests. While the representation of minority interests is a critical aspect of any democratic system, de Tocqueville does not mention it as a source of conflict in these excerpts. His concern lies with the majority's potential to misuse their power, not with the representation of minority interests. D) Legislators sometimes act against majority rule. De Tocqueville does not highlight this as a potential source of conflict in these excerpts. He focuses on the potential misuse of power by the majority, rather than on instances where legislators may act against the majority's wishes. Conclusion According to Alexis de Tocqueville in "Democracy in America", the potential for the majority to misuse its power and obtain special rights is a possible source of conflict in the U.S. system of government. He does not identify the minority's ability to block legislation, the requirement for legislators to represent minority interests, or legislators acting against majority rule as potential sources of conflict.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: The United States fought in World War II from 1941 to 1945. Which statement explains the peak annual inflation rate during the 1940s?
Choice A: Consumer demand increased due to the end of rationing.
Choice B: Manufacturing decreased because of less demand for weapons.
Choice C: Government spending increased due to national defense.
Choice D: Wages decreased because of competition for scarce jobs.
Rationale:
The word 'entrenched' in this context means 'established and unlikely to change'.

This interpretation is supported by the excerpt's description of the party system in America. The term 'entrenched' suggests that the system has become deeply embedded and stable over time, showing resistance to alteration or removal. A) in control While it is true that political parties can be in control of a government or political system, this does not encapsulate the full meaning of 'entrenched'. Entrenched indicates a level of permanence or resistance to change, which is not necessarily implied by being 'in control'. B) strongly defended While 'entrenched' could mean 'strongly defended' in certain contexts, such as in military terminology, this is not the primary meaning in this context. The excerpt does not discuss any defense or protection of the party system, but rather its deep-rooted and enduring presence in American politics. C) established and unlikely to change This choice correctly interprets 'entrenched' as it is used in the excerpt. The party system is described as being deeply ingrained, or established, in American politics, and given its long history and the behaviors of American voters, it is unlikely to change significantly in the future. D) surrounded and unable to move This interpretation of 'entrenched' is more in line with its military usage, where it describes forces that are dug in and surrounded, unable to advance or retreat. This does not fit the usage in the excerpt, which describes the party system as being firmly established and resistant to change, rather than immobilized or surrounded. Conclusion In the given context, 'entrenched' denotes the state of being deeply established and resistant to change. The party system in America, as described in the excerpt, has become a deeply rooted and enduring aspect of the nation's political landscape, unlikely to undergo significant alteration. Other interpretations of 'entrenched', such as being in control, strongly defended, or surrounded and unable to move, do not accurately capture the full meaning of the term in this context.

Exam Name: Free Social Studies GED Practice Test
Question Text: Which statement is supported by both the table and the map?
Choice A: Differences in the total populations of the states led to political differences between the North and the South.
Choice B: Differences among the economies of the states contributed to political differences between the North and the South.
Choice C: Political differences between the North and the South reflected differences in education among the states.
Choice D: Political differences between the North and the South reflected differences in the standards of living in the states.
Rationale:
"To the contrary, the government they devised was defective from the start, requiring several amendments, a civil war, and momentous social transformation to attain the system of constitutional government, and its respect for the individual freedoms and human rights, we hold as fundamental today."

This statement directly implies that the original U.S. government, as established by the Constitution, did not adequately protect the rights of all individuals, particularly those in minority groups. It suggests that significant changes and struggles, including amendments, a civil war, and a major societal shift, were necessary to reach the level of individual freedom and human rights protections that are considered foundational in modern times. A) "To the contrary, the government they devised was defective from the start, requiring several amendments, a civil war, and momentous social transformation to attain the system of constitutional government, and its respect for the individual freedoms and human rights, we hold as fundamental today." This statement directly addresses the original deficiencies in the U.S. government's protection of individual and minority rights. It outlines specific historical events and processes that were necessary to amend these deficiencies, thus providing evidence for the inference that minority rights were not initially protected by the U.S. government. B) "In a very real sense, it was then, in 1787, that the Revolution truly began." This statement refers to the beginnings of the Revolution and the year the Constitution was written, but it does not provide specific evidence about the protection (or lack thereof) of minority rights in the original governmental structure. C) "I do not believe that the meaning of the Constitution was forever 'fixed' at the Philadelphia Convention." This statement suggests that the Constitution has evolved and changed over time. However, it does not directly provide evidence about the initial protection of minority rights by the U.S. government. D) "For it was with the writing of our Constitution, setting down the architecture of democratic government, that the noble sentiments and brave rhetoric of 1776 took on substance......" African Americans dealt with Jim Crow laws by seeking factory jobs in urban areas of the North.
